title: IBM Outlines Sub-1nm Nanostack Transistor Technology: Building the Next Gen By Going Up
gist: IBM Research has publicly announced the Nanostack, a next-generation transistor design targeting sub-1nm (7 Angstrom) geometries that uses vertical wafer stacking to continue shrinking transistors beyond the limits of current GAAFET technology. IBM believes Nanostack will serve as the foundation for fab node technologies for at least a decade, arriving as GAAFET is projected to exhaust its scalability in the early-to-mid 2030s.

themes.theme: Moore's Law Slowdown and Transistor Scaling Crisis

insights.claim: The pace of silicon transistor shrinkage has slowed from a 50% reduction every two years over nearly 50 years to roughly every five years in the current decade.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: The deceleration of Moore's Law increases the importance of architectural innovation and raises costs per compute gain, affecting semiconductor capex and AI chip economics.
insights.quote: Compared to the nearly 50-year span of being able to shrink transistors by 50% every two years, in this decade the pace of change is closer to every five years

insights.claim: The pace of progress is especially poor for SRAM and analog logic designs, which lag behind even the general slowdown in silicon lithography.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: SRAM scaling bottlenecks constrain cache density in CPUs and AI accelerators, potentially driving architectural workarounds like near-memory or in-package DRAM.
insights.quote: things are even worse for some designs such as SRAM and analog logic

insights.claim: IBM Research has developed a transistor design called the Nanostack, aimed at sub-1nm geometries — specifically the 7 Angstrom (7Å) generation.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: IBM's Nanostack positions the company as a leading research actor in post-GAAFET transistor technology, with potential licensing or foundry partnership implications.
insights.quote: IBM Research is publicly announcing that they have developed a next-generation transistor design to carry the industry in the next decade, which they are calling the Nanostack

insights.claim: The Nanostack design uses vertical wafer stacking to build complete logic circuits upward rather than shrinking horizontally, enabling smaller and denser transistors.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Vertical integration of transistor layers may create new equipment supply chain demand (deposition, bonding tools) and shift competitive advantage toward fabs with advanced 3D integration capabilities.
insights.quote: By stacking parts of a complete logic circuit on top of each other and building up in the vertical direction, IBM believes that the technology will not only unlock the ability to reliably manufacture smaller circuits

insights.claim: IBM believes the Nanostack stacking approach can be extended to further layers to achieve even greater transistor densities beyond the initial 7Å node.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Multi-layer extensibility suggests Nanostack could serve as a long-duration platform rather than a transitional node, reducing industry R&D fragmentation risk.
insights.quote: stacking can be extended to further layers for even greater transistor densities

insights.claim: IBM projects that Nanostack transistors will be the basis of fab node technologies for at least a decade.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: A decade-long roadmap anchored to a single architecture provides capital planning visibility for fabs and chip designers investing in next-generation tooling.
insights.quote: nanostack transistors will be the basis of fab node technologies for at least a decade

themes.theme: Current Transistor Technology: FinFET to GAAFET Transition

insights.claim: The semiconductor industry's three major fabs — TSMC, Intel, and Samsung — are currently transitioning from FinFET to Gate All Around (GAAFET) transistors.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: The GAAFET transition represents a major capex cycle already underway at all three leading fabs, with implications for equipment vendors like ASML and Applied Materials.
insights.quote: the big three chip fabs – TSMC, Intel, and Samsung – are all in the process of switching from FinFET transistors to Gate All Around (GAAFET) transistors

insights.claim: FinFET transistors, used by Intel since 2012, have run out of headroom due to electron leakage that cannot be controlled at modern process nodes.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: FinFET exhaustion is a confirmed forcing function driving both the GAAFET transition and longer-term Nanostack research, validating the need for continuous transistor architecture reinvention.
insights.quote: FinFET transistors, which have been with the industry since Intel started using them in 2012, have largely run out of headroom

insights.claim: GAAFET transistors place a gate fully surrounding the channel to reduce electron leakage, and use multiple stacked nanosheets per transistor to improve performance.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Understanding GAAFET as a nanosheet architecture clarifies the technical lineage to IBM's Nanostack, which extends vertical stacking to the full circuit level.

insights.claim: GAAFET reached high volume manufacturing only recently: Samsung with SF3E, Intel with 18A, and TSMC with N2.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: All three leading fabs are at the beginning of their GAAFET volume ramp, meaning returns on GAAFET capex are still early and the next-generation transition is 5-7 years away.
insights.quote: GAAFET process nodes have just reached high volume manufacturing in the last couple of years (Samsung with SF3E, Intel with 18A, and TSMC with N2)

themes.theme: GAAFET Limits and the Post-GAAFET Timeline

insights.claim: As GAAFET nanosheets thin out at smaller process nodes, they will eventually fail to contain electron leakage, limiting GAAFET's ultimate scalability.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: GAAFET's structural leakage problem at extreme scaling is the direct technical motivation for IBM's Nanostack, confirming the post-GAAFET research race is not premature.
insights.quote: as these sheets thin out, they ultimately will struggle to contain leakage once again

insights.claim: Imec, the leading independent nanoelectronics research organization, projects that GAAFET will run its course in the early-to-mid 2030s, leaving only 5-7 years for cutting-edge use.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: The Imec roadmap provides a credible external anchor for the post-GAAFET transition timeline, making IBM's Nanostack readiness window clearly defined and strategically urgent.
insights.quote: currently the best guess as pegged by Imec...is that GAAFET will run its course in the early-to-mid 2030s

insights.claim: The 5-7 year commercial viability window for GAAFET means research organizations must now refine GAAFET's successor to be ready for commercial production in the next decade.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: The compressed timeline from GAAFET ramp to post-GAAFET readiness compresses the window for research-to-production and may accelerate consolidation among fabs with viable next-gen paths.
insights.quote: on that short of a timeline, that means it is already time for research organizations to be refining its successor so that it can be ready for commercial production in the next decade
